critic_raw:
This experiment tests a bidirectional LSTM versus logistic regression for predictive maintenance (equipment failure prediction) using SCADA sensor data.

The 7-day prediction window is used for both the positive label definition and the LSTM lookback window. This is a direct structural overlap: the same 168-hour window defines what constitutes a positive example and also constitutes the LSTM's entire input sequence. This means the LSTM's input is always centered on the 7-day pre-failure window for positive examples. In contrast, for negative examples, the 168-hour window is chosen from non-failure periods. The model may learn to distinguish these two types of windows based on properties unrelated to failure dynamics — for example, if operators perform pre-failure inspection procedures that alter sensor readings in the 7 days before a failure is logged. The window choice creates a behavioral change in the system that is itself correlated with the label, independent of any mechanical failure signal.

The logistic regression baseline uses the same 9 engineered features 'averaged or summarized over the 168-hour window,' but the RNN sees the full sequential structure of those 9 features over 168 timesteps. This collapses from 168×9=1,512 values to 9 summary statistics. The comparison is not between architectures applied to the same information — it is between architectures with fundamentally different input representations. Any performance difference is as likely to reflect the information in the full sequence not captured by averages as it is to reflect LSTM-specific temporal modeling.

The primary metric is F1 score tuned on the validation set. For predictive maintenance, the costs of false positives (unnecessary maintenance) and false negatives (unplanned downtime) are typically asymmetric and domain-specific. A single F1 score implicitly assumes equal cost weighting. The design acknowledges this with precision and recall as secondary metrics, but the model selection and primary claim are made on F1. If the plant manager's actual cost ratio is 5:1 (missed failure costs 5x more than false alarm), the F1-optimized model may be suboptimal by a large margin without any signal in the primary metric.

Machine type stratification ensures each machine type appears in all splits. However, individual machine age (time-since-installation) is not mentioned as a feature or stratification variable. Machine degradation is a function of operating hours and maintenance history, not calendar time. A machine installed in month 1 of the study period will be 36 months old at test time while its failure dynamics will differ from a machine installed in month 24. This age confound is not addressed.
all_issues_raised: The 7-day failure prediction window and the 7-day LSTM lookback window are identical, meaning positive examples' input sequences are always drawn from the exact pre-failure period. Operator behavior changes (pre-failure inspections, maintenance prep) may be detectable in sensor data within this window, causing the model to exploit behavioral artifacts rather than mechanical failure dynamics.

The logistic regression baseline collapses 168 timesteps × 9 features into 9 summary statistics, while the LSTM processes the full 1,512-value sequence. This is an information asymmetry, not just an architectural one.

F1 as the primary metric implicitly assumes symmetric costs for false positives (unnecessary maintenance) and false negatives (unplanned downtime). For predictive maintenance, these costs are typically asymmetric, and F1 optimization may select models that are suboptimal under realistic cost structures.

Machine age (time-since-installation) is not included as a feature or stratification variable. Machines of different ages have different failure dynamics; the training set will include younger machines whose test-period failure patterns differ from older machines entering higher-risk phases.
